Within a group (vertical column), atomic radius increases with increasing atomic number. For example, in Group 1A (alkali metals), the outermost electron is in an \(ns\) orbital. As you go down the group from Li to Cs, the principal quantum number (n) increases, meaning the orbital size gets larger. Even though the effective nuclear charge also increases, the increasing number of electron shells makes the atoms larger.

1A 2A 3A 4A 5A 6A 7A 8A
H 37 He
31
Li Be B C N O F Ne
152 112 85 77 75 73 72 70
Na Mg Al Si P S Cl Ar
186 160 143 118 110 103 99
K Ca Ga Ge As Se Br Kr
227 197 135 123 120 117 114 112
Rb Sr In Sn Sb Te I Xe
248 215 166 140 141 143 133 131
Cs Ba Tl Pb Bi Po At Rn
265 222 171 175 155 164 142 140

Example 2: Trend Across a Period

Across a period (horizontal row), atomic radius generally decreases from left to right. This is because as you move across a period, the nuclear charge increases, pulling the outer electrons closer to the nucleus, even though the number of electron shells remains the same.

For instance, consider N, P, and Si. N and P are in the same group (Group VA), so P is larger than N. Si and P are in the same period, with Si to the left of P, so Si is larger than P. Therefore, the order of increasing atomic radius is \(N < P < Si\).

2.2.5.2 Ionization Energy

Ionization energy (IE) is the minimum energy required to remove an electron from an isolated gaseous atom in its ground electronic state, forming a cation. This energy is usually expressed in kJ/mol.

\(H(g) \rightarrow H^+(g) + e^-\)

General Trend: Ionization energy generally increases from left to right across a period and decreases down a group.

Example 1: Successive Ionization Energies

Removing the first electron (first IE) requires less energy than removing the second electron (second IE), and so on. This is because after the first electron is lost, the atom becomes a positive ion, increasing the attraction for the remaining electrons, making them harder to remove. The more electrons lost, the stronger the positive charge and the harder it is to separate subsequent electrons.

Element Li Be B C N O F
Ionization Energy (kJ/mol) 520 899 800 1086 1402 1314 1680

Example 2: Effect of Atomic Radius on IE

The further an electron is from the nucleus (larger atomic radius), the easier it is to expel, resulting in a lower ionization energy. Conversely, a smaller radius means electrons are held tighter, requiring more energy to remove.

Consider Oxygen (O) and Sulfur (S). Both are in Group VIA. Sulfur's 3p electron is further from the nucleus than Oxygen's 2p electron, experiencing less nuclear attraction. Thus, Sulfur has a smaller first ionization energy than Oxygen.

Exceptions:

  • Group IIA elements often have higher ionization energy than Group IIIA elements because their completely filled s-orbitals are very stable.
  • Group VA elements often have higher ionization energy than Group VIA elements because their half-filled p-orbitals are more stable.
  • Alkali metals (Group IA) have low ionization energies, while halogens and Group VIIIA elements (noble gases) have high ionization energies.
Element Li Na K Rb Cs Fr
Ionization Energy (kJ/mol) 520 496 419 408 376 396

Shielding Effect: Inner electrons shield outer electrons from the full positive charge of the nucleus. A higher shielding effect leads to a lower ionization energy. This is why ionization energy decreases down a group. Cesium has the lowest ionization energy, and Fluorine (with the exception of Helium and Neon) has the highest.

2.2.5.3 Electron Affinity

Electron affinity (EA) is the energy change (in kJ/mol) when an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom to form a negative ion. It reflects an atom's likelihood of gaining an electron.

\(X(g) + e^- \rightarrow X^-(g)\) (First Electron Affinity - negative energy released)

\(X^-(g) + e^- \rightarrow X^{2-}(g)\) (Second Electron Affinity - positive energy needed)

When an electron is added to a neutral atom (first electron affinity), energy is typically released (negative value). However, adding an electron to an already negative ion (second electron affinity) usually requires energy (positive value) due to electron-electron repulsion.

Example 1: Halogen Electron Affinities

Electron affinities are mostly discussed for elements in Groups VI and VII. Halogens (Group VIIA) have high electron affinities because they need only one electron to achieve a stable octet. For instance, the first electron affinity of chlorine is -349 kJ/mol, indicating energy is released when chlorine gains an electron.

  • Fluorine (F): -328 kJ/mol
  • Chlorine (Cl): -349 kJ/mol
  • Bromine (Br): -324 kJ/mol
  • Iodine (I): -295 kJ/mol

Notice that electron affinity generally decreases down a group (due to increasing atomic size and weaker attraction for the incoming electron), but increases across a period (as atoms get smaller and nuclear charge increases).

Example 2: Irregularity in Electron Affinity

Why is the electron affinity of Fluorine less than that of Chlorine? Fluorine has five electrons in its 2p-subshell, while Chlorine has five in its 3p-subshell. The 3p-subshell is larger than the 2p-subshell. Because fluorine is smaller, there is more electron-electron repulsion in its 2p-shell than in chlorine's 3p-shell. This greater repulsion means fluorine is less able to accommodate an incoming electron than chlorine, resulting in less energy being released, hence a lower electron affinity.

Element Li Be B C N O F
Electron Affinity (kJ/mol) -60 0 -27 -122 0 -141 -328

When an electron is added to a metal element, energy is needed (endothermic reaction), as metals prefer to lose electrons to form cations. Thus, metals have lower electron affinities. Nonmetals, with more valence electrons and smaller sizes, have higher electron affinities.

Element Li Na K Rb Cs
Electron Affinity (kJ/mol) -60 -53 -48 -47 -45

2.2.5.4 Electronegativity

Electronegativity is an atom's tendency in a molecule to attract a shared pair of electrons towards itself. It is a dimensionless property, a measure of an atom's "pull" on bonding electrons. The most common scale, developed by Linus Pauling, gives fluorine the highest electronegativity (4.0) and cesium the lowest (0.7).

Trends in Electronegativity:

  • Across a Period: Electronegativity increases from left to right. As nuclear charge increases and atomic size decreases, the pull on bonding electrons becomes stronger.
  • Down a Group: Electronegativity decreases down a group. Although nuclear charge increases, the added electron shells increase atomic size, reducing the attraction for bonding electrons.

Example 1: Fluorine vs. Cesium

Fluorine is the most electronegative element (4.0) because it has a small size and a strong nuclear pull. Cesium (0.7) and francium (0.7) are the least electronegative elements due to their large atomic sizes and weaker nuclear pull on valence electrons.

Example 2: Metals vs. Nonmetals

Metals generally have lower electronegativity values, making them electropositive (tend to lose electrons). Nonmetals, on the other hand, have higher electronegativity, making them electronegative (tend to gain electrons).

2.3 Atomic Structure and Periodic Properties of the Elements

2.3.1 Historical Development of Atomic Structure

The philosophical ideas about the composition of matter date back to ancient Greece. Philosophers like Plato and Aristotle believed matter was continuous, meaning it could be divided infinitely into smaller pieces. However, Democritus (460-370 BC) challenged this idea, proposing that matter eventually breaks down into tiny, indestructible particles called atomos, or "atoms," which means "indivisible."

Democritus's ideas were based purely on speculation rather than experimental evidence, and they weren't widely accepted until 1808. After nearly 2000 years, John Dalton developed an atomic theory that gained broad scientific acceptance. Dalton's theory was revolutionary because it was based on observations and helped explain fundamental laws of chemistry.

2.3.2 Dalton's Atomic Theory and the Modern Atomic Theory

Dalton's atomic theory, developed in the early 19th century, was a groundbreaking step in understanding matter. It was built upon two crucial scientific laws:

  • Law of Conservation of Mass: This law states that mass is neither created nor destroyed during a chemical reaction. In simpler terms, the total mass of the reactants before a chemical reaction must equal the total mass of the products after the reaction. For example, if you burn a piece of wood in a sealed container, the total mass of the ash, smoke, and gases produced will be the same as the original mass of the wood and the oxygen consumed.
  • Law of Definite Proportions: Also known as the law of constant composition, this law states that a chemical compound always contains exactly the same proportion of elements by mass, regardless of the size or source of the sample. For instance, pure water (\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)) will always be composed of 11.2% hydrogen and 88.8% oxygen by mass.
  • The law of multiple proportion: When two elements form more than one compound between them, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in small whole-number ratios.

    Example: Carbon and oxygen form both carbon monoxide (\(\text{C}\text{O}\)) and carbon dioxide (\(\text{C}_2\text{O}\)).

    • In (\(\text{C}\text{O}\)), 12 g of carbon combines with 16 g of oxygen.
    • In (\(\text{C}_2\text{O}\)), 12 g of carbon combines with 32 g of oxygen.

      • The ratio of oxygen masses that combine with the same mass of carbon = 16:32 = 1:2 (a small whole-number ratio).

Drawbacks (limitations) of Dalton’s Atomic Theory:

    Atoms are not indivisible

  • Dalton said atoms can not be divided, but we now know they contain protons, neutrons, and electrons.

    • Dalton claimed all atoms of an element have the same mass and properties, but atoms of different elements must have different masses.

  • Discovery of isotopes (same element, different masses) disproved this.
  • Isobars (different elements with the same mass number) show this is not always true.

    • So, atoms of different elements can have the same mass

Based on these laws, Dalton formulated his atomic theory, which included several key postulates:

  1. All matter is composed of extremely small, indivisible particles called atoms.
  2. Atoms of a given element are identical in mass and properties; atoms of different elements have different masses and properties.
  3. Atoms cannot be created, destroyed, or subdivided in chemical reactions.
  4. Atoms of different elements combine in simple whole-number ratios to form chemical compounds.
  5. In chemical reactions, atoms are combined, separated, or rearranged.

Example 1: Law of Definite Proportions in Water

Water always contains 11.2% hydrogen and 88.8% oxygen by mass.

a. If you have 18.0 g of water, how much hydrogen and oxygen can be obtained?

  • Mass of hydrogen = \(0.112 \times 18.0 \text{ g} = 2.02 \text{ g}\)
  • Mass of oxygen = \(0.888 \times 18.0 \text{ g} = 15.98 \text{ g}\)

b. If you have 1.00 g of water, how much hydrogen and oxygen can be obtained?

  • Mass of hydrogen = \(0.112 \times 1.00 \text{ g} = 0.112 \text{ g}\)
  • Mass of oxygen = \(0.888 \times 1.00 \text{ g} = 0.888 \text{ g}\)

This demonstrates that the ratio of hydrogen to oxygen by mass remains constant regardless of the total amount of water.

Example 2: Law of Multiple Proportions

The law of multiple proportions states that if two elements can combine to form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other element are in ratios of small whole numbers. Let's look at nitrogen and oxygen compounds:

Compound Mass of nitrogen that combines with 1 g of oxygen
A 1.750 g
B 0.8750 g
C 0.4375 g

To show this illustrates the law of multiple proportions, we compare the ratios of nitrogen masses for a fixed mass of oxygen (1 g):

  • Ratio A:C = \(\frac{1.750 \text{ g}}{0.4375 \text{ g}} = \frac{4}{1}\)
  • Ratio B:C = \(\frac{0.8750 \text{ g}}{0.4375 \text{ g}} = \frac{2}{1}\)

Since the ratios (4:1 and 2:1) are small whole numbers, these data support the law of multiple proportions.

i. Postulates of Modern Atomic Theory

While Dalton's atomic theory was a monumental achievement, later discoveries refined our understanding of the atom. The modern atomic theory acknowledges that atoms are not truly indivisible but are composed of subatomic particles. It also accounts for isotopes and nuclear reactions.

Most of the experiments that led to the modern atomic theory are discussed in later sections. Dalton's work primarily focused on how atoms combine to form compounds, not their internal structure. The theories about the physical structure of atoms truly began with J.J. Thomson's discovery of the electron in 1897.

2.3.3 Early Experiments to Characterize the Atom

i. The Discovery of the Electron

In 1897, the English physicist J.J. Thomson conducted pioneering experiments with cathode rays, which are beams of particles emitted from the negative electrode (cathode) in a vacuum tube when a high-voltage current is applied. These rays interact with the glass tube, causing a greenish light. Thomson observed that these cathode rays bent away from a negatively charged plate and towards a positively charged plate, and their characteristics were independent of the material of the cathode.

From these observations, Thomson concluded that cathode rays consist of a beam of negatively charged particles, which he called electrons, and that these electrons are fundamental constituents of all matter.

Thomson was able to calculate the ratio of the electron's mass (\(m_e\)) to its charge (\(e\)). He found this ratio to be approximately \(-5.686 \times 10^{-12} \text{ kg C}^{-1}\). However, he couldn't determine the mass or charge separately.

In 1909, American physicist Robert A. Millikan successfully measured the charge of an electron through his famous oil-drop experiment. He found the charge to be \(e = -1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}\). Using this value and Thomson's mass/charge ratio, Millikan calculated the mass of an electron: \[m_e = \] \[(-5.686 \times 10^{-12} \text{ kg C}^{-1}) \times (-1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) =\] \[9.109 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}\]

ii. Radioactivity and the Discovery of the Nucleus

Radioactivity (or radioactive decay) is the spontaneous emission of particles and/or radiation from the unstable nuclei of certain atoms, like uranium and radium. The discovery of radioactivity further challenged Dalton's idea of indivisible atoms.

Soon after radioactivity was discovered, three types of emissions were identified from radioactive substances:

  • Alpha (\(\alpha\)) rays: These consist of positively charged particles (\(\alpha\)-particles). An \(\alpha\)-particle has a mass about four times that of a hydrogen atom and a charge twice the magnitude of an electron. They are identical to helium nuclei.
  • Beta (\(\beta\)) rays: These are electrons (\(\beta\)-particles) originating from inside the nucleus. They are deflected by a negatively charged plate.
  • Gamma (\(\gamma\)) rays: These are high-energy rays with no charge and are not affected by external electric or magnetic fields.

In 1911, J.J. Thomson proposed the "plum-pudding" model of the atom, suggesting that electrons ("plums") were randomly distributed within a positively charged cloud ("pudding"). However, Ernest Rutherford and his co-workers tested this model with their famous gold foil experiment.

In Rutherford's experiment, positively charged \(\alpha\)-particles were aimed at a thin sheet of gold foil. If the plum-pudding model were correct, the \(\alpha\)-particles should have passed straight through with minimal deflection. To Rutherford's great surprise, some particles were deflected at large angles, and a few even bounced back in the opposite direction.

This unexpected observation led Rutherford to propose a new model for the atom: the nuclear atom. He concluded that an atom consists of a tiny, dense, positively charged center called the nucleus, with electrons orbiting around it. Most of the atom is empty space, which is why most \(\alpha\)-particles passed through the gold foil undeflected. The few particles that were deflected or bounced back did so because they hit or passed very close to the small, massive, positively charged gold nuclei.

iii. Discovery of the Neutron

The neutron, another subatomic particle, was discovered later through alpha-particle scattering experiments. In 1932, British physicist James Chadwick (1891-1974) showed that when beryllium metal is irradiated with alpha rays, a strongly penetrating radiation is produced. This radiation consists of neutral particles. A neutron is a nuclear particle with a mass almost identical to that of a proton but with no electric charge. Its mass, \(m_n = 1.67493 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}\), is about 1840 times the mass of an electron.

2.3.4 Make-up of the Nucleus

In 1919, Rutherford discovered that hydrogen nuclei, which he called protons, are formed when alpha particles strike lighter elements like nitrogen. A proton is a nuclear particle with a positive charge equal in magnitude to that of an electron. Its mass, \(m_p = 1.67262 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}\), is about 1840 times the mass of electrons. Protons give the nucleus its positive charge.

The atomic number (Z) of an element is equal to the number of protons in the nucleus of each of its atoms. All atoms of a particular element have the same atomic number, and each element has a unique atomic number. The mass number (A) is the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus of an atom. The mass number and atomic number of an element X are often written as \(_{Z}^{A}X\).

The term atomic mass unit (amu) is used for relative masses of subatomic particles, where 1 amu is defined as \(\frac{1}{12}\) the mass of a carbon-12 atom.

Proton (p): Has an actual mass of \(1.672622 \times 10^{-27}\) kg (relative mass 1.007276 amu) and an actual charge of \(1.602 \times 10^{-19}\) C (relative charge +1).

Neutron (n): Has an actual mass of \(1.674927 \times 10^{-27}\) kg (relative mass 1.008665 amu) and carries no charge (actual 0 C, relative 0).

Electron (e): Has an actual mass of \(9.109383 \times 10^{-31}\) kg (relative mass \(5.485799 \times 10^{-4}\) amu) and an actual charge of \(-1.602 \times 10^{-19}\) C (relative charge -1).

i. Atomic Mass and Isotopes

Not all atoms of an element are identical in mass number, even though they have the same atomic number. Isotopes are atoms of an element that have different numbers of neutrons and, therefore, different mass numbers.

For example, all carbon atoms have six protons (\(Z = 6\)). However, 98.89% of naturally occurring carbon atoms have six neutrons (\(A = 12\)), while 1.11% have seven neutrons (\(A = 13\)), and a very small percentage (less than 0.01%) have eight neutrons (\(A = 14\)). Thus, carbon has three naturally occurring isotopes: \(^{12}\text{C}\), \(^{13}\text{C}\), and \(^{14}\text{C}\).

Most elements in nature are mixtures of isotopes. The atomic mass of an element is the average mass of its isotopes, weighted by their observed fractional abundances. If an element has \(n\) isotopes with relative masses \(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\) and fractional abundances \(f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n\), the average relative atomic mass (\(A\)) is calculated as: \[A = A_1f_1 + A_2f_2 + \ldots + A_n f_n\]

Example 1: Calculating Atomic Mass of Silver

Silver has two naturally occurring isotopes: \(^{107}\text{Ag}\) (106.90509 amu) with 51.84% abundance, and \(^{109}\text{Ag}\) (108.90476 amu) with 48.16% abundance. Calculate the atomic mass of silver.

Solution:

  • Portion of atomic mass from \(^{107}\text{Ag}\) = Isotopic mass \(\times\) Fractional abundance
  • Portion of atomic mass from \(^{107}\text{Ag}\) = \(106.90509 \text{ amu} \times 0.5184 = 55.42 \text{ amu}\)
  • Portion of atomic mass from \(^{109}\text{Ag}\) = \(108.90476 \text{ amu} \times 0.4816 = 52.45 \text{ amu}\)
  • Atomic mass of Ag = \(55.42 \text{ amu} + 52.45 \text{ amu} = 107.87 \text{ amu}\)

Example 2: Isotopic Abundance Calculation

Naturally occurring boron consists of two isotopes, \(^{10}\text{B}\) (10.013 amu) and \(^{11}\text{B}\) (11.009 amu). The observed atomic mass of boron is 10.811 amu. Calculate the abundance of each isotope.

Solution:

Let \(x\) be the fractional abundance of \(^{10}\text{B}\). Then the fractional abundance of \(^{11}\text{B}\) is \( (1 - x) \).

Atomic Mass = \( (\text{Mass of }^{10}\text{B} \times x) + (\text{Mass of }^{11}\text{B} \times (1 - x)) \)

\(10.811 = (10.013 \times x) + (11.009 \times (1 - x))\)

\(10.811 = 10.013x + 11.009 - 11.009x\)

\(10.811 - 11.009 = 10.013x - 11.009x\)

\(-0.198 = -0.996x\)

\(x = \frac{-0.198}{-0.996} \approx 0.1988\)

So, the abundance of \(^{10}\text{B}\) is \(0.1988 \times 100\% = 19.88\%\).

The abundance of \(^{11}\text{B}\) is \( (1 - 0.1988) \times 100\% = 80.12\%\).

2.3.5 Electromagnetic Radiation and Atomic Spectra

i. Electromagnetic Radiation

In 1873, James Clerk Maxwell proposed that light consists of electromagnetic waves. These waves have both an electric field and a magnetic field component, which vibrate perpendicular to each other and to the direction of wave propagation. Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR) is the emission and transmission of energy in the form of these electromagnetic waves.

Electromagnetic waves are characterized by three primary properties:

  • Wavelength (\(\lambda\), Greek lambda): This is the distance a wave travels during one complete cycle. It's often expressed in meters (m), nanometers (nm), picometers (pm), or angstroms (Å).
  • Frequency (\(\nu\), Greek nu): This is the number of wave cycles that pass a given point per second. It's expressed in units of 1/second (\(\text{s}^{-1}\)), also known as hertz (Hz).
  • Speed (\(c\)): The speed of a wave depends on the type of wave and the medium it travels through. In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves travel at a constant speed of \(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}\), which is called the speed of light.

The relationship between these properties is given by the equation: \[c = \nu\lambda\]

EMR encompasses a wide range of frequencies, collectively known as the electromagnetic spectrum. This spectrum includes radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. Visible light, which appears as a continuous spectrum (like a rainbow), ranges from red (\(\lambda = 750 \text{ nm}\)) to violet (\(\lambda = 380 \text{ nm}\)).

Radiation is a crucial means of energy transfer. For instance, the sun's energy reaches Earth as visible and ultraviolet radiation, while a fireplace transmits heat as infrared radiation.

Example 1: Wavelength of a Radio Wave

Ethiopian National Radio, Addis Ababa station broadcasts its AM signal at a frequency of 2400 kHz. What is the wavelength of the radio wave expressed in meters?

Solution:

First, convert the frequency to Hz: \(2400 \text{ kHz} = 2400 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}\).

Using the formula \(c = \nu\lambda\), we can rearrange to find wavelength: \(\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu}\)

\(\lambda = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ s}^{-1}} = 125.0 \text{ m}\)

Example 2: Frequency of Earth's Radiation

The most intense radiation emitted by the Earth has a wavelength of about 10.0 \(\mu\)m. What is the frequency of this radiation in hertz?

Solution:

First, convert the wavelength to meters: \(10.0 \text{ \(\mu\)m} = 10.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}\).

Using the formula \(c = \nu\lambda\), we can rearrange to find frequency: \(\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}\)

\(\nu = \frac{3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{10.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}} = 3.00 \times 10^{13} \text{ Hz}\)

ii. The Quantum Theory and Photon

In 1900, German physicist Max Planck introduced a revolutionary idea: energy, like matter, is discontinuous. He proposed that atoms and molecules can only emit or absorb energy in discrete, tiny packets called quanta. The energy (\(E\)) of a single quantum is directly proportional to the frequency (\(\nu\)) of the radiation:

\[E = h\nu\]

Where \(h\) is Planck's constant, with a value of \(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}\). Since \(\nu = c/\lambda\), this equation can also be written as: \[E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\]

This means energy is always emitted or absorbed in integral multiples of \(h\nu\) (e.g., \(h\nu\), \(2h\nu\), \(3h\nu\), etc.). This concept suggests that energy has particulate properties.

a. The Photoelectric Effect

In 1905, Albert Einstein used Planck's quantum theory to explain the photoelectric effect, a phenomenon where electrons are ejected from the surface of certain metals when exposed to light of at least a certain minimum frequency, called the threshold frequency (\(\nu_0\)).

These observations led to the understanding that EMR is quantized, meaning it consists of particles called photons, which are packets of light energy. The minimum energy required to remove an electron from a metal's surface is related to the threshold frequency:

\[E_0 = h\nu_0\]

If a photon's energy is less than \(E_0\) (i.e., \(\nu < \nu_0\)), no electrons will be ejected. If the photon's energy is greater than \(E_0\), the excess energy is converted into the kinetic energy (KE) of the ejected electron:

\[\text{KE}_e = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = h\nu - h\nu_0\]

Here, \(\text{KE}_e\) is the kinetic energy of the electron, \(m\) is its mass, \(v\) is its velocity, \(h\nu\) is the energy of the incident photon, and \(h\nu_0\) is the energy required to remove the electron (also called the work function).

The intensity of light relates to the number of photons. A higher intensity means more photons are available, leading to more electrons being released (provided \(\nu > \nu_0\)).

Einstein's theory of relativity also showed that energy has mass, given by \(E = mc^2\). This implies that a photon, despite having no rest mass, has an apparent mass when moving, which can be expressed as: \[m = \frac{E}{c^2} = \frac{h\nu}{c^2} = \frac{h}{c\lambda}\]

This illustrates the dual nature of light: it can exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties.

Example 1: Energy of Blue Light

The blue color in fireworks is often achieved by heating copper (I) chloride (CuCl) to about 1200 °C, which then emits blue light with a wavelength of 600 nm. What is the increment of energy (the quantum) that is emitted by CuCl at 600 nm?

Solution:

First, convert wavelength to meters: \(600 \text{ nm} = 600 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 6.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}\).

Calculate the frequency (\(\nu\)):

\(\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} =\) \(\frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{6.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}}\) \(= 0.5 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}\) \(= 5.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}\)

Now, calculate the energy (\(E\)):

\(E = h\nu\) \(= (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}) \) \(= 3.315 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}\)

Example 2: Photoelectric Effect Calculations

The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from a given metal is \(1.5 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}\) when light with a 750 nm wavelength shines on the surface. Determine the threshold frequency (\(\nu_0\)) for this metal and the corresponding wavelength (\(\lambda_0\)).

Solution:

a) Determination of the threshold frequency (\(\nu_0\)):

First, calculate the frequency (\(\nu\)) of the incident light:

\(\lambda = 750 \text{ nm} = 7.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}\)

\(\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{7.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}} = 4.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}\)

From the photoelectric effect equation: \(\text{KE}_e = h\nu - h\nu_0\), we can solve for \(h\nu_0\):

\(h\nu_0 = h\nu - \text{KE}_e\)

\(\nu_0 = \frac{h\nu - \text{KE}_e}{h}\) \(= \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}) \times (4.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}) - (1.5 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J})}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}}\)

\(\nu_0 = \frac{2.652 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} - 1.5 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}} = \frac{2.402 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}}\)

\(\nu_0 = 3.623 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}\)

b) Calculate the corresponding wavelength (\(\lambda_0\)):

\(\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{\nu_0} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{3.623 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1}} = 8.28 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}\) \(= 828 \text{ nm}\)

iii. Atomic Spectra

When an electric discharge (or spark) passes through a gas, like hydrogen, it excites the atoms. The electric current transfers energy to the electrons, raising them to higher energy states. When these excited electrons return to lower energy states, they emit the absorbed energy in the form of light. This emitted light, when passed through a prism, does not produce a continuous spectrum (like a rainbow) but rather a series of individual colored lines. This series of lines is called an element's atomic spectrum or emission spectrum.

Each element has a unique atomic spectrum, and the specific wavelengths of these spectral lines are characteristic of the element, making them useful for identification. For example, the hydrogen line spectrum shows only a few discrete wavelengths, not a continuous band of colors. This indicates that electrons in atoms can only exist at specific, discrete energy levels.

Changes in energy between these discrete energy levels produce only certain wavelengths of emitted light. A given change in energy (\(\Delta E\)) from a high to a lower energy level corresponds to the emission of a photon with a specific wavelength, which can be calculated using Planck's equation: \[\Delta E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\]

iv. The Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom

In 1913, Danish physicist Niels Bohr proposed a model for the hydrogen atom that explained why orbiting electrons do not continuously radiate energy. He introduced the fundamental idea that the absorption and emission of light by hydrogen atoms are due to discrete energy changes of electrons within the atoms. The fact that only certain frequencies are absorbed or emitted confirms that energy changes in an atom are quantized.

Bohr's model incorporated Planck's and Einstein's ideas about quantized energy and made the following key assumptions:

  1. Electrons in a hydrogen atom travel around the nucleus in circular orbits.
  2. The energy of an electron in an atom is proportional to its distance from the nucleus. Electrons further from the nucleus have more energy.
  3. Only a limited number of orbits with specific energies are allowed. These orbits are quantized.
  4. The only allowed orbits are those for which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of \(\frac{h}{2\pi}\). This is called the quantum condition: \[mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi}\]
  5. As long as an electron stays in a given orbit, it neither gains nor loses energy. The atom's energy remains constant while the electron moves within that orbit.
  6. An electron moves to a higher energy orbit by absorbing energy (in the form of light) and emits light when it falls to a lower energy orbit. The energy of the absorbed or emitted photon is exactly equal to the difference between the energies of the two orbits.

Bohr showed that the radii (\(r\)) of the permitted orbits or energy levels for a hydrogen atom are related to Planck's constant (\(h\)), the electron's charge (\(e\)), and its mass (\(m\)). The formula for the radius is:

\[r = n^2 a_0\]

Where \(n\) is a positive integer (\(n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\)) called the quantum number, and \(a_0\) is the Bohr radius, with a numerical value of 0.53 Å. This means the allowed radii are proportional to the square of a whole number. For instance, for \(n=1\), \(r = 0.53 \text{ Å}\) (the first Bohr radius).

Bohr also derived an equation for the energies an electron can occupy in a hydrogen atom:

\[E_n = -\frac{R_H}{n^2}\]

Where \(R_H\) is the Rydberg constant for hydrogen (\(2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}\)). The negative sign indicates that the electron's energy in the atom is lower than that of a free electron (which is assigned zero energy). The lowest energy state (\(n = 1\)) is called the ground state, and higher energy states (\(n = 2, 3, \ldots\)) are called excited states.

The change in energy (\(\Delta E\)) when an electron moves between energy levels \(n_i\) (initial) and \(n_f\) (final) is:

\[\Delta E = E_f - E_i = -R_H \left(\frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2}\right)\]

If \(n_i > n_f\), \(\Delta E\) is negative, meaning energy is emitted. If \(n_f > n_i\), \(\Delta E\) is positive, meaning energy is absorbed.

Example 1: Bohr Model Calculations for Hydrogen Atom

Consider the \(n = 5\) state of a hydrogen atom. Using the Bohr model, calculate the radius of the electron orbit, the velocity, and the energy of the electron.

Solution:

a) To determine the radius (\(r\)), use the equation \(r = n^2 a_0\):

\(r = (5^2) \times 0.53 \text{ Å} = 25 \times 0.53 \text{ Å} = 13.25 \text{ Å}\) \(= 1.325 \text{nm}\)

b) Velocity (\(v\)) can be determined from the angular momentum equation, \(mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi}\), rearranging for \(v\): \[v = \frac{nh}{2\pi mr}\] Given: \(n=5\), \(h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}\), \(m=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}\), \(r=1.325 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}\) (converted from Å) \[v = \] \[\frac{5 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}}{2 \times 3.14 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg} \times 1.325 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}\] \[= 4.37 \times 10^5 \text{ m/s}\]

c) To calculate the energy (\(E_n\)) for \(n=5\), use the equation \(E_n = -\frac{R_H}{n^2}\):

\(E_5 = -\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{5^2} = -\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{25}\) \(= -8.72 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}\)

Example 2: Energy and Wavelength of Emitted Light

Calculate the energy emitted when an electron moves from the \(n = 3\) to the \(n = 2\) energy level in a hydrogen atom. Determine the wavelength of the emitted energy.

Solution:

To determine the emitted energy (\(\Delta E\)):

\(\Delta E = -R_H \left(\frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2}\right)\) \(= -2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} \left(\frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2}\right)\)

\(\Delta E = -2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}\right)\) \(= -2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} \left(\frac{9-4}{36}\right)\)

\(\Delta E = -2.18 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} \left(\frac{5}{36}\right)\) \(= -3.028 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}\)

The negative sign indicates that energy is emitted.

To obtain the wavelength (\(\lambda\)):

\(\Delta E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \implies \lambda = \frac{hc}{|\Delta E|}\)

\(\lambda = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}) \times (3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{3.028 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}\)

\(\lambda = 6.56 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 656 \text{ nm}\)

a. Limitations of the Bohr Model

While the Bohr model was a significant advance, it had several limitations:

  • It could only accurately explain the atomic spectra of hydrogen and other one-electron species (like \(\text{He}^+\) and \(\text{Li}^{2+}\)). It failed for more complicated atoms and ions.
  • It could not explain the further splitting of spectral lines when atoms were placed in a magnetic field (the Zeeman effect).
  • It assumed electrons have both a precisely known radius and orbit, which contradicts Heisenberg's uncertainty principle.

v. The Wave-Particle Duality of Matter and Energy

Bohr's assumption of fixed energy levels lacked a physical basis. In 1924, French physicist Louis de Broglie proposed a groundbreaking idea: if energy can be particle-like (photons), perhaps matter can be wavelike. He reasoned that if electrons exhibit wavelike motion and are restricted to orbits of fixed radii, this would explain why they have only certain possible frequencies and energies.

De Broglie combined Einstein's equation for energy-mass equivalence (\(E = mc^2\)) with Planck's equation for photon energy (\(E = h\nu = hc/\lambda\)) to derive an equation for the wavelength of any particle with mass (\(m\)) moving at speed (\(v\)): \[\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}\]

This equation, known as the de Broglie wavelength, indicates that both matter and energy possess both wave and particulate properties. This concept is called wave-particle duality. At the macroscopic level, the wavelike properties of objects are imperceptible due to their large mass and extremely small wavelengths. However, at the atomic and subatomic levels, this duality is highly significant.

Example 1: Wavelength of an Electron and a Ball

Compare the wavelength for an electron (mass = \(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}\)) traveling at a speed of \(1.00 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s}\) with that for a ball (mass = \(0.10 \text{ kg}\)) traveling at 35 m/s.

Solution:

We use de Broglie's equation: \(\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}\), where \(h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}\) (or \(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ kg \(\cdot\) m}^2\text{/s}\)).

For the electron:

\(\lambda_e = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ kg \(\cdot\) m}^2\text{/s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (1.00 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s})}\) \(= 7.28 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 0.0728 \text{ nm}\)

For the ball:

\(\lambda_{\text{ball}} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ kg \(\cdot\) m}^2\text{/s}}{(0.10 \text{ kg}) \times (35 \text{ m/s})}\) \(= 1.89 \times 10^{-34} \text{ m} = 1.89 \times 10^{-25} \text{ nm}\)

The electron's wavelength is measurable, while the ball's wavelength is astronomically small, illustrating why wave properties are not observed for macroscopic objects.

Example 2: Speed of an Electron with a Given Wavelength

What is the speed of an electron that has a de Broglie wavelength of 1.00 nm?

Solution:

Rearrange de Broglie's equation to solve for velocity: \(v = \frac{h}{m\lambda}\)

Given: \(\lambda = 1.00 \text{ nm} = 1.00 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}\), \(m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}\), \(h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J \(\cdot\) s}\).

\(v = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ kg \(\cdot\) m}^2\text{/s}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}) \times (1.00 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m})} = 7.28 \times 10^5 \text{ m/s}\)

2.3.6 The Quantum Mechanical Model of the Atom

The Bohr model, while explaining the hydrogen spectrum, couldn't account for atomic properties beyond hydrogen or for the precise location and velocity of electrons. This led to the development of quantum mechanics, which describes electrons around the nucleus based on their wave behavior and the uncertainty principle.

i. The Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle

The wave properties of matter posed a challenge: if subatomic particles behave like waves, their precise location is not well-defined. In 1927, German physicist Werner Heisenberg proposed the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle, which states that it is impossible to simultaneously know with perfect precision both the position (\(\Delta x\)) and the momentum (\(\Delta p\)) of an electron (or any particle). Mathematically, it's expressed as:

\[(\Delta x)(\Delta p) \geq \frac{h}{4\pi} \quad \text{or} \quad (\Delta x)(m\Delta v) \geq \frac{h}{4\pi}\]

This means if you measure the momentum of a particle very precisely (\(\Delta p\) is small), its position becomes less certain (\(\Delta x\) becomes larger), and vice versa. In the macroscopic world, this principle has no practical consequence, but for atomic and subatomic particles, it is profoundly significant.

ii. Quantum Numbers

The quantum mechanical description of the atom assumes that both matter and radiation have associated waves. Erwin Schrödinger (1927) developed a mathematical equation, called the wave function (\(\psi\)), which describes the wavelike properties of electrons. While complex, its solutions describe the allowed energy states of electrons.

The square of the wave function, \(\psi^2\), represents the probability of finding an electron in a specific region of space around the nucleus. This region of highest probability is called an orbital. Electrons move rapidly within an orbital, behaving like an "electron cloud" with varying density.

In quantum mechanics, each electron in an atom is described by a unique set of four quantum numbers:

  1. Principal Quantum Number (\(n\)):
    • Describes the main energy level, or shell, an electron occupies.
    • It can be any positive integer: \(n = 1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots\).
    • Determines the size and primary energy of the orbital. It's analogous to the energy levels in Bohr's model.
  2. Angular Momentum Quantum Number (\(l\)):
    • Also called the azimuthal quantum number or subsidiary quantum number.
    • Designates the shape of the atomic orbitals and defines subshells within a shell.
    • It takes integral values from 0 to \(n-1\).
    • Each \(l\) value corresponds to a specific orbital shape and is denoted by letters:
      Value of \(l\) Corresponding subshell
      0 s
      1 p
      2 d
      3 f
  3. Magnetic Quantum Number (\(m_l\)):
    • Also called the orbital-orientation quantum number.
    • Has integral values ranging from \(-l\) to \(+l\), including 0.
    • Describes the orientation of the orbital in space. The number of possible \(m_l\) values (and thus orbitals) for a given \(l\) value is \(2l + 1\).
  4. Electron Spin Quantum Number (\(m_s\)):
    • Refers to the intrinsic angular momentum of an electron, which creates a magnetic field.
    • Can take one of two values: \(+\frac{1}{2}\) or \(-\frac{1}{2}\). These represent the two possible spin orientations (often visualized as "spin up" and "spin down").
    • Each atomic orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons, one with \(m_s = +\frac{1}{2}\) and the other with \(m_s = -\frac{1}{2}\).

Example 1: Identifying Quantum Numbers in 4d

What do "4" and "d" represent in 4d?

Solution:

  • The number "4" represents the principal quantum number (\(n\)), indicating the fourth main energy level.
  • The letter "d" represents the angular momentum quantum number (\(l\)), specifically \(l=2\), which corresponds to a d-orbital shape.

Example 2: Allowed Quantum Numbers for \(n=3\)

What values of the angular momentum quantum number (\(l\)) and magnetic quantum number (\(m_l\)) are allowed for a principal quantum number, \(n = 3\)? How many orbitals are allowed for \(n = 3\)?

Solution:

Determining \(l\) values:

  • For \(n = 3\), \(l\) can take values from 0 to \(n-1\). So, \(l = 0, 1, 2\).

Determining \(m_l\) for each \(l\) value:

  • For \(l = 0\): \(m_l = 0\) (This corresponds to one 3s orbital).
  • For \(l = 1\): \(m_l = -1, 0, +1\) (This corresponds to three 3p orbitals).
  • For \(l = 2\): \(m_l = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2\) (This corresponds to five 3d orbitals).

Number of orbitals for \(n = 3\):

  • Total number of orbitals for a given \(n\) is \(n^2\).
  • For \(n = 3\), the total number of orbitals is \(3^2 = 9\) orbitals.
  • These are: 1 (3s) orbital + 3 (3p) orbitals + 5 (3d) orbitals = 9 orbitals.

iii. Shapes of Atomic Orbitals

The three-dimensional aspects and orientation of atomic orbitals are typically visualized using boundary surface diagrams. These diagrams enclose the region where there is about a 90% probability of finding an electron.

  • s-orbitals (\(l=0\)):

    S-orbitals are spherically symmetrical, meaning the electron density is uniform in all directions around the nucleus. The 1s orbital is the smallest, the 2s orbital is larger than the 1s, and so on. All s-orbitals, regardless of their principal quantum number, maintain this spherical shape.

  • p-orbitals (\(l=1\)):

    P-orbitals are dumbbell-shaped. For any principal quantum number (starting from \(n=2\)), there are three p-orbitals, each oriented along one of the three mutually perpendicular axes: \(p_x\), \(p_y\), and \(p_z\). Each p-orbital has two lobes lying along an axis, with the nucleus at the center.

  • d-orbitals (\(l=2\)):

    D-orbitals exist from \(n=3\) onwards and are more complex in shape than p-orbitals. There are five d-orbitals. Four of them have four lobes arranged in different orientations (e.g., \(d_{xy}, d_{yz}, d_{xz}, d_{x^2-y^2}\)), while the fifth (\(d_{z^2}\)) has a unique shape consisting of two lobes along the z-axis and a donut-shaped ring around the middle.

  • f-orbitals (\(l=3\)):

    F-orbitals exist from \(n=4\) onwards. There are seven f-orbitals, and their shapes are even more intricate and complex than d-orbitals.

2.3.7 Electronic Configurations and Orbital Diagrams

The electron configuration of an atom describes the distribution of electrons among its atomic orbitals. This arrangement follows three fundamental principles:

  1. Aufbau (Building Up) Principle:

    This principle states that electrons fill atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy, from the lowest-energy orbital to the highest. This "building up" order generally follows a specific sequence:

    1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d, 5p, 6s, 4f, 5d, 6p, 7s, 5f, 6d, 7p

    Electrons will always occupy the lowest available energy orbital before moving to a higher one. This is crucial for determining the ground state electron configuration of an atom.

  2. Hund's Rule:

    When filling degenerate orbitals (orbitals of equal energy, such as the three p-orbitals or five d-orbitals), electrons will first occupy each orbital singly with parallel spins before any orbital is occupied by a second electron with opposite spin. For example, for the three 2p orbitals (\(2p_x, 2p_y, 2p_z\)), each will receive one electron before any of them gets a second electron.

  3. Pauli's Exclusion Principle:

    This principle states that no two electrons in the same atom can have the exact same set of four quantum numbers (\(n, l, m_l, m_s\)). This means that an atomic orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons, and these two electrons must have opposite spins (one \(+\frac{1}{2}\) and one \(-\frac{1}{2}\)).

i. Ground State Electronic Configuration of the Elements

There are two main ways to represent electron configurations:

  • Subshell (or sublevel) Notation: This uses numbers for principal energy levels, and letters (s, p, d, f) for sublevels. A superscript indicates the number of electrons in that sublevel. For example, the ground state electron configuration of hydrogen (Z=1) is \(1s^1\).
  • Orbital Diagram: This uses boxes (or circles/lines) to represent orbitals and arrows to indicate electrons. An upward arrow (\(\uparrow\)) usually denotes \(m_s = +\frac{1}{2}\), and a downward arrow (\(\downarrow\)) denotes \(m_s = -\frac{1}{2}\).
Atom Atomic number (Z) Electron configuration
H 1 \(1s^1\)
He 2 \(1s^2\)
Be 4 \(1s^22s^2\) or \([He]2s^2\)
O 8 \(1s^22s^22p^4\) or \([He]2s^22p^4\)
Ne 10 \(1s^22s^22p^6\) or \([He]2s^22p^6\)
Cl 17 \(1s^22s^22p^63s^23p^5\) or \([Ne]3s^23p^5\)
Ar 18 \(1s^22s^22p^63s^23p^6\) or \([Ne]3s^23p^6\)
Ca 20 \(1s^22s^22p^63s^23p^64s^2\) or \([Ar]4s^2\)
Sc 21 \(1s^22s^22p^63s^23p^64s^23d^1\) or \([Ar]4s^23d^1\)
Cr 24 \(1s^22s^22p^63s^23p^64s^13d^5\) or \([Ar]4s^13d^5\)

In an atom's electron configuration, electrons in the outermost principal quantum level are called valence electrons. These are the most important electrons for chemists because they are involved in chemical bonding. For example, in Be, the \(2s\) electrons are valence electrons. In Ne, the \(2s\) and \(2p\) electrons are valence electrons. Electrons in inner shells are called core electrons.

There are some exceptions to the Aufbau principle, particularly among transition elements like chromium (Cr) and copper (Cu). The expected configurations do not always match the observed ones, which are determined through emission spectra and magnetic properties. These exceptions usually occur when half-filled or fully-filled d-subshells offer special stability.

Element Expected Configuration Observed Configuration
Cr (Z = 24) \([Ar]4s^23d^4\) \([Ar]4s^13d^5\)
Cu (Z = 29) \([Ar]4s^23d^9\) \([Ar]4s^13d^{10}\)

This stability arises because the energy difference between \(4s\) and \(3d\) orbitals is very small. At higher principal quantum numbers, these energy differences can be even smaller, leading to more exceptions among heavier transition elements.

2.3.8 Electronic Configurations and the Periodic Table of the Elements

The modern periodic table arranges elements in a specific pattern that helps predict their properties and highlights similarities and differences. This arrangement is based on the periodic law, which states that certain physical and chemical properties of elements repeat at regular intervals (periodically) when the elements are arranged according to increasing atomic number.

i. The Modern Periodic Table

The periodic table organizes elements into periods (horizontal rows) and groups (vertical columns).

  • A period indicates the value of the principal quantum number (\(n\)) for the outermost (valence) shell.
  • Groups consist of elements with similar outer-shell electron configurations, which explains their similar chemical behavior.

The modern periodic table has 18 groups and 7 periods. Elements can be broadly classified as:

  • Representative (Main-Group) Elements: These are elements in the s-block and p-block (Groups 1, 2, and 13-18). Their valence electrons are in s and p orbitals.
  • Transition Elements: These are elements in the d-block (Groups 3-12). Their inner d-orbitals are being filled.
  • Inner-Transition Elements: These are the lanthanides and actinides (f-block elements), typically placed separately at the bottom of the table. Their inner f-orbitals are being filled.

Metalloids are elements that possess properties intermediate between metals and non-metals (e.g., boron, germanium, silicon).

ii. Periodic Properties

The electron configurations of atoms cause a periodic variation in properties as the atomic number increases. These include:

  • Atomic Radii: This is an estimate of the size of an atom. Generally, atomic radius increases down a group (due to increasing \(n\) and more electron shells) and decreases across a period from left to right (due to increasing effective nuclear charge, \(Z_{\text{eff}}\), which pulls valence electrons closer to the nucleus).
  • Ionization Energy (IE): This is the energy required to remove 1 mole of electrons from 1 mole of gaseous atoms or ions. The first ionization energy (\(\text{IE}_1\)) is the energy needed to remove the first electron. Generally, ionization energy decreases down a group (easier to remove electrons further from the nucleus) and increases across a period (harder to remove electrons due to increasing \(Z_{\text{eff}}\)). There are small irregularities due to electron configurations (e.g., half-filled or full subshells provide extra stability).
  • Electron Affinity (EA): This is the energy change when an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom to form a negative ion. A negative EA value means energy is released (the process is exothermic), indicating the atom has an attraction for an electron. Generally, electron affinity becomes more negative from left to right across a period (atoms tend to accept electrons more readily).
  • Electronegativity: This is a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons in a chemical bond. Linus Pauling assigned fluorine (the most electronegative element) a value of 4.0 on the Pauling scale. Electronegativity generally increases across a period (as \(Z_{\text{eff}}\) increases) and decreases down a group (as atomic radius increases).
  • Metallic Character: This refers to the chemical properties associated with metals, primarily their ability to lose electrons. Metallic character decreases across a period from left to right (as atoms tend to gain electrons) and increases down a group (as electrons are easier to lose due to larger atomic radii and weaker nuclear attraction).

Example 1: Atomic Size Trends

Rank the following sets of main-group elements in order of decreasing atomic size:

a. Ca, Mg, Sr

b. K, Ga, Ca

c. Br, Rb, Kr

Solution:

a. For Ca, Mg, Sr (Group 2): Atomic size increases down a group. So, Sr > Ca > Mg.

b. For K, Ga, Ca (Period 4): Atomic size decreases across a period. K (Group 1) is largest, then Ca (Group 2), then Ga (Group 13). So, K > Ca > Ga.

c. For Br, Rb, Kr: Rb (Period 5, Group 1) is the largest. Br (Period 4, Group 17) is smaller than Rb but larger than Kr (Period 4, Group 18). So, Rb > Br > Kr.

Example 2: Ionization Energy Trends

Based on their positions in the periodic table, predict which atom of the following pairs will have the larger first ionization energy:

a. Ga or Ge

b. Br or Sb

c. K or Cr

Solution:

a. Ge will have a larger ionization energy than Ga. Ge is to the right of Ga in the same period, meaning it has a higher effective nuclear charge and a smaller atomic radius, making it harder to remove an electron.

b. Br will have a larger ionization energy than Sb. Br is above Sb in the same group and is also located to the right of Sb in the periodic table relative to its period. Moving across a period increases IE, while moving down decreases it. Br is in Period 4 and Group 17, while Sb is in Period 5 and Group 15. The general trend is increasing IE to the right and decreasing down, placing Br higher than Sb.

c. Cr will have a larger ionization energy than K. Cr is to the right of K in the same period, making it harder to remove an electron due to increased effective nuclear charge.

iii. Advantages of Periodic Classification of the Elements

The periodic classification of elements offers several advantages:

  • It is based on the atomic number, a fundamental property of an element, providing a systematic and accurate classification.
  • Isotopes are naturally averaged in one place within the table, as classification is based on atomic number, not mass.
  • It explains the periodicity of element properties and links them directly to their electronic configurations.
  • It correctly justified the positions of elements that were "misfits" based on mass number (e.g., argon and potassium), aligning them correctly by atomic number.
  • The lanthanides and actinides are logically placed separately at the bottom, maintaining the table's overall structure.
  • The periodic table is a simple, systematic, and easy way to remember and understand the properties of various elements due to its organization by electron configuration.

Key concepts Summary

  • The atomic structure has been developed through discoveries of the atom and fundamental subatomic particles (protons, neutrons, electrons) and the nucleus.
  • Key terms like atomic number, mass number, isotope, energy level, valence electrons, and electronic configuration are crucial for understanding atomic structure.
  • We can calculate the number of protons, electrons, and neutrons from atomic and mass numbers.
  • The calculation of atomic masses for elements with isotopes involves considering their natural abundance.
  • Electrons are arranged in main energy levels (shells) around the nucleus, defining an atom's electronic configuration.
  • Atomic size: Half the distance between nuclei of adjacent atoms.
  • Electron affinity: Energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom.
  • Electronegativity: An atom's tendency to attract shared electrons in a molecule.
  • Group: Vertical column in the periodic table; elements have similar properties.
  • Ionization energy: Energy required to remove an electron from an atom.
  • Law of octaves: Newlands' rule where properties repeat every eighth element.
  • Law of triads: Dobereiner's rule for groups of three elements with similar properties.
  • Periodic law: Properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic number.
  • Period: Horizontal row in the periodic table; elements arranged by increasing atomic number.
  • Atomic Theory: All matter is composed of atoms. Dalton's theory laid the foundation, explaining conservation of mass and definite/multiple proportions.
  • Subatomic Particles: Electrons (discovered by Thomson), protons (discovered by Rutherford), and neutrons (discovered by Chadwick) are the building blocks of atoms.
  • Atomic Number (Z): Number of protons, defines an element.
  • Mass Number (A): Total number of protons and neutrons.
  • Isotopes: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons and thus different mass numbers.
  • Electromagnetic Radiation (EMR): Energy transmitted as waves with wavelength (\(\lambda\)), frequency (\(\nu\)), and speed (\(c\)). Related by \(c = \nu\lambda\).
  • Quantum Theory: Energy is quantized into discrete packets called photons, with energy \(E = h\nu\).
  • Photoelectric Effect: Emission of electrons from a metal surface when light of sufficient frequency strikes it, explained by the photon concept.
  • Bohr Model: Described electrons orbiting the nucleus in fixed energy levels (quantized), explaining the hydrogen atomic spectrum.
  • Wave-Particle Duality: Matter and energy exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties, described by de Broglie's equation (\(\lambda = h/mv\)).
  • Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle: Impossible to simultaneously know both the precise position and momentum of a particle.
  • Quantum Numbers: Four numbers (\(n, l, m_l, m_s\)) that describe the state of an electron in an atom.
  • Atomic Orbitals: Regions of space around the nucleus where an electron is most likely to be found (s-spherical, p-dumbbell, d-complex).
  • Electron Configuration: Distribution of electrons in orbitals, governed by Aufbau principle, Hund's rule, and Pauli's exclusion principle.
  • Periodic Law: Properties of elements repeat periodically when arranged by increasing atomic number.
  • Periodic Properties: Trends in atomic size, ionization energy, electron affinity, electronegativity, and metallic character based on position in the periodic table.

Quiz Complete! 🎉
